title: Why Diamonds Need Extreme Pressure to Form

lesson.learn:
Diamonds are simply carbon arranged under the right combination of heat and pressure. Deep inside the Earth, carbon can be squeezed into a tightly bonded crystal structure that is much harder than graphite. When those diamonds are brought to the surface, they can survive because the crystal lattice is stable, even though the conditions that made them are extreme.

This is why diamonds are so fascinating. They are ordinary atoms arranged in an extraordinary structure, formed far below where most people ever look.
lesson.simpler: Diamonds form when carbon is forced into a tight crystal structure under intense pressure deep underground.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Diamond is a form of carbon that needs very special conditions to become stable.
lesson.deeperDive:
The key idea is that carbon has more than one crystal form. Graphite is stable at the surface, but diamond becomes stable at much higher pressures. That means depth matters. Most natural diamonds form deep in the mantle and are later transported upward by fast-moving volcanic rocks such as kimberlite.

The transition is a good example of how chemistry and geology meet. The same element can become a very different mineral when pressure changes, and the crystal structure is what gives diamond its famous hardness and brilliance.
lesson.funFact: Diamond and graphite are both made of carbon.

people: Geologists - people who study deep Earth conditions
Diamond miners - people who find diamond-bearing rock
Material scientists - researchers who study hardness
places: Kimberlite pipes - fast pathways to the surface
Mining regions - where diamonds are recovered
Laboratories - where carbon phases are studied
years: Present day - diamond formation is understood through phase diagrams
concepts: Carbon - the element diamonds are made of
Crystal structure - the arrangement of atoms
Pressure - the force that changes carbon form
Graphite - the softer carbon form at the surface
Kimberlite - a rock that carries diamonds upward
